content:
13 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

When generating code becomes cheap, architecture becomes the control system. The challenge is no longer knowing who wrote the code — it is designing boundaries, rules, and evidence that keep the system coherent regardless.AI is great at writing code, but it’s terrible at maintaining architectural integrity. Stop relying on human vigilance and start building “Golden Paths,” blast-radius boundaries, and executable policies to keep your codebase from becoming an archaeological site.
A few months ago, I opened a pull request that looked completely normal.
The tests passed. The feature worked. The naming was decent. The code was even documented, which should have been my first warning.
Then I tried to understand why the implementation had three layers of abstraction around a function that converted one string into another.
There was a factory.
The factory created a strategy.
The strategy selected an adapter.
The adapter called trim().
Nothing was technically wrong.
That was the problem.
A human engineer would probably have become embarrassed somewhere around the second interface. An AI coding tool has no such survival mechanism. It does not get tired of maintaining unnecessary abstractions. It does not worry that the rest of the team will make fun of it at lunch. It can generate another 600 lines before you’ve finished saying, “Actually, maybe we don’t need a provider registry here.”
That PR changed something in the way I think about architecture.
For most of my career, software architecture quietly assumed that humans were the primary producers of code.
Humans have limited working memory. Humans get bored. Humans avoid writing twelve slightly different implementations because copying them twelve times is annoying. Humans remember that the billing system has a weird exception because three years ago it ruined their weekend.
AI-generated code changes several of those assumptions.
And that means the question is no longer simply:
How do we write good code with AI?
The more interesting question is:
What does good software architecture look like when a significant percentage of the code may be generated by systems that can produce code much faster than humans can understand it?
That is the real architecture problem.
Not whether Copilot uses the correct loop syntax.
Not whether Claude can build a CRUD endpoint.
Not whether your IDE agent can refactor a React component while you make coffee.
The problem is containing the consequences of cheap code generation.
Because when producing code becomes dramatically cheaper, the scarce resource moves somewhere else.
That scarce resource is now increasingly human comprehension, architectural integrity, and the ability to verify that 50 individually reasonable changes still produce one coherent system.
After working with AI-assisted coding across real codebases, I’ve become much more opinionated about what “architecture for AI-generated code” should optimize for.
These are the five patterns I keep coming back to.
The old architectural assumptions are quietly expiring
Traditional architecture has always accounted for human limitations.
We create modules because nobody can hold an entire system in their head.
We use conventions because developers forget things.
We automate tests because humans make mistakes.
We perform code review because the person who wrote the code has context that others don’t.
But there were also assumptions we rarely wrote down.
We assumed a developer would notice when they had implemented the same concept five times.
We assumed someone adding a dependency probably understood why it existed.
We assumed code volume had a natural economic constraint because someone had to type, debug, and maintain it.
We assumed the author had at least some memory of the architectural decisions made earlier in the week.
AI coding tools weaken all four assumptions.
They can generate thousands of plausible lines without feeling architectural discomfort. They can introduce a new pattern because it appeared statistically appropriate in the current context window. They can solve the same problem differently in neighboring modules because those modules were generated in different sessions.
This doesn’t make AI-generated code bad.
I’ve seen AI produce excellent implementations, catch bugs I missed, and eliminate hours of mechanical work.
But the economics are different.
When code becomes cheap, inconsistency becomes cheap too.
So architecture has to become less dependent on the judgment of whoever — or whatever — happens to be writing the next function.
Pattern 1: The Golden Path Architecture
A Golden Path Architecture makes the correct implementation path dramatically easier than inventing a new one.
This matters more with AI-generated code because AI coding agents are extremely good at following strong local precedent — and extremely creative when precedent is ambiguous.
Give an agent three different ways to build an API endpoint and you may soon have a fourth.
Give it one obvious template, one shared library, one directory structure, and one tested example, and the probability of architectural improvisation drops dramatically.
Suppose your application has a standard request flow:
Route
-> Input validation
-> Application service
-> Domain operation
-> Repository
-> Response mapperDon’t merely describe that structure in a 47-page architecture document nobody has opened since the Christmas party.
Encode it into the repository.
For example:
src/
modules/
orders/
order.routes.ts
order.schema.ts
order.service.ts
order.repository.ts
order.mapper.tsProvide one reference implementation that is considered canonical.
Provide shared primitives:
export function createHandler(schema, useCase) {
return async (req, res) => {
const input = schema.parse(req.body);
const result = await useCase(input);
return res.json(result);
};
}Now an AI agent creating customers, invoices, or subscriptions has rails to run on.
Without those rails, you’ll eventually discover that one module uses repositories, another uses direct ORM calls, another has invented a “DomainGatewayManager,” and one particularly enthusiastic agent has rediscovered CQRS because you asked it to add a field to a form.
The architecture should make boring consistency the path of least resistance.
This isn’t about eliminating flexibility.
It’s about making architectural deviation an explicit decision rather than an accidental side effect of code generation.
The practical move is simple: identify your five or ten most common implementation tasks and create canonical examples for them.
An agent should not need to decide how your system performs logging, validation, authorization, database access, error handling, or event publication every time it writes code.
You’ve already made those decisions.
Make them executable.
The funny version is that your architecture now needs the equivalent of those airport moving walkways: you can still walk in another direction, but you should have to work noticeably harder to do it.
Pattern 2: Blast-Radius Boundaries
The second pattern is designing modules so that generated mistakes fail locally.
I call these Blast-Radius Boundaries.
AI-generated code increases the importance of this pattern because the danger is rarely one catastrophic line of nonsense.
The more realistic danger is a perfectly reasonable implementation that misunderstood one assumption and propagated that misunderstanding through six services.
Imagine an AI agent is asked:
Add support for cancelling an order.
It finds this:
await orderRepository.update(id, {
status: "cancelled"
});Looks reasonable.
Except your business rules say an order cannot be cancelled after warehouse dispatch, payments must be refunded, stock must be restored, and partners must receive an event.
If business invariants live primarily in developer memory, AI-generated code will eventually bypass them.
Instead, put the boundary around the operation:
await order.cancel({
requestedBy: userId,
reason
});Inside the domain boundary:
if (this.status === "shipped") {
throw new OrderCannotBeCancelled();
}this.status = "cancelled";
this.raise(new OrderCancelled(this.id));
Now the calling code has fewer ways to be accidentally correct-looking.
The principle is:
Generated code should interact with capabilities, not manipulate internal state.
This applies beyond domain-driven design.
Your payment module should expose refundPayment(), not unrestricted database access.
Your permissions system should expose can(user, action, resource), not invite every feature to reinterpret roles.
Your storage layer should expose bounded file operations instead of handing application code a raw S3 client.
Your tenant-aware database layer should make cross-tenant queries difficult by construction.
Every boundary reduces the amount of global knowledge required to implement something safely.
That is valuable for junior developers.
It is even more valuable for AI agents, which may have perfect recall of the current file and absolutely no awareness of the architectural scar tissue created by the incident of March 2023.
The analogy I use is watertight compartments on a ship.
You’re not claiming the ship will never leak.
You’re deciding that one confused piece of code should not sink payroll, authentication, inventory, and the coffee machine simultaneously.
Pattern 3: Architecture as Executable Policy
For years, many teams treated architecture rules as documentation.
“Services should not access controllers.”
“Domain code should not depend on infrastructure.”
“Frontend components should use the API client.”
“Don’t import Prisma directly into application services.”
These rules often lived in a wiki.
Which is architecturally equivalent to writing “PLEASE DO NOT ENTER” on a piece of paper and placing it next to an open vault.
With AI-generated code, architectural rules need to become executable policy.
If a dependency is forbidden, make the build fail.
If an import is illegal, make linting fail.
If an API contract changes unexpectedly, make CI fail.
If a database migration violates your rules, reject it automatically.
For example, suppose your architecture is:
presentation -> application -> domain
|
v
infrastructureYou can enforce this with dependency rules rather than trusting every contributor to remember the diagram.
Conceptually:
forbidImport({
from: "domain",
to: ["infrastructure", "presentation"]
});forbidImport({
from: "application",
to: ["presentation"]
});
The exact tooling varies by language and ecosystem.
The principle does not.
Turn architectural intent into machine-checkable constraints wherever possible.
Other useful examples include:

• Schema validation at every external boundary.

• Consumer-driven contract tests between services.

• API specification checks in CI.

• Dependency allowlists.

• Maximum module dependency depth.

• Automated detection of circular dependencies.

• Migration policies that reject destructive operations without explicit approval.

• Security scanners that block known-dangerous patterns.
This matters because AI coding agents respond extremely well to fast feedback.
If an agent generates an illegal dependency and CI says:
ERROR: domain/orders cannot import infrastructure/databasethe agent can usually fix it immediately.
If the feedback arrives six weeks later during an architecture review when somebody asks, “Why does OrderEntity import StripeClient?”, the conversation becomes more philosophical.
And nobody wants a philosophical conversation about StripeClient at 4:45 PM on Friday.
Architectural governance needs to move closer to compilation.
The more code is generated, the less scalable it becomes to enforce architecture through human vigilance alone.
Pattern 4: Replace Code Review with Evidence Review
I’m deliberately being provocative with the name.
Humans should still review code.
But traditional code review often assumes that reading the diff is the primary way t
